register_rationale: Tracy is a Senior Program Manager with 15+ years of experience and a university degree. FK grade 13 professional prose is appropriate — outcome-focused framing with executive register, compound sentences acceptable, no heavy academic abstraction.

reading_level.vocabulary_rule: Technical terms may appear without inline definition if in glossary; academic prose OK; compound sentences acceptable. Expert PM vocabulary assumed.

vocabulary_substitutions.report: executive status report or meeting summary
vocabulary_substitutions.meeting: stand-up, leadership review, or project sync
vocabulary_substitutions.file: Google Drive file or meeting recording
vocabulary_substitutions.project: one of Tracy's two concurrent programs

domain_protagonist.name: Tracy
domain_protagonist.key_pain_point: manually synthesizing information from 10+ systems into weekly executive reports — a process that consumes hours she could spend on strategic work

domain_systems: Google Drive (meeting recordings, Docs, Sheets, folders)

ch01-intro-to-cowork-automation.scenario_title: Weekly project status report from scattered sources
ch01-intro-to-cowork-automation.rationale: Establishes the core problem Tracy is solving — manual aggregation of Jira, Confluence, Slack, and meeting transcripts into a status report — grounding Chapter 1's conceptual overview in her exact Monday-morning pain point.

ch02-automation-mindset.rationale: Chapter 2 uses Tracy's transcript-to-summary pain point to teach workflow decomposition: a raw transcript is a structured input; a summary with decisions/action items/risks is a structured output. Tracy can immediately map the framework to her Tuesday post-meeting routine.

ch03-setting-up-claude.rationale: Workspace setup is motivated by the inbox problem — Tracy needs a configured Claude environment and organized session outputs before she can run a triage workflow. Setting up file handling, session management, and organized outputs is directly preparatory to the email triage workflow.

ch04-context-management.rationale: Context quality determines whether Claude can correctly propose a folder hierarchy and rename hundreds of files without hallucinating. Chapter 4's context management principles (reference docs, structured inputs, avoiding overloaded conversations) are taught through Tracy's Drive reorganization task.

ch05-claude-md.rationale: CLAUDE.MD persistent instructions encode Tracy's output standards for meeting summaries: who owns action items, how decisions are formatted, which project terminology to use. The transcript summarization scenario is a natural CLAUDE.MD use case Tracy can build in Chapter 5 and reuse immediately.

ch06-creating-skills.scenario_title: Weekly project status report from scattered sources
ch06-creating-skills.rationale: The status report workflow is Tracy's highest-value automation candidate. Chapter 6 builds her first reusable skill — the status report aggregation skill — using this scenario as the design brief, aligning the chapter's 'first reusable skill' deliverable with her stated primary learning goal.

ch07-evaluating-skills.rationale: Tracy needs a reliable evaluation rubric for her transcript summarization skill before she can trust it over Gemini auto-notes. Chapter 7's accuracy, completeness, format, usefulness, and risk checks are applied to a meeting summary output — a high-stakes artifact that goes to leadership.

ch08-connecting-tools.rationale: Connecting to Gmail via a plugin or MCP tool is the prerequisite for automated inbox triage. Chapter 8's safe import checklist and tool integration map are built around Tracy's Gmail + label + archive workflow, establishing trust and permissions before the automation runs.

ch09-playwright-browser-automation.rationale: Google Drive reorganization requires browser-based navigation — clicking through folders, reading file metadata, applying renames — when no Drive MCP is available. Chapter 9's browser automation workflow and human-review checkpoints are taught through Tracy's Drive cleanup task.

ch10-zapier-saas-automation.rationale: Zapier enables trigger-action workflows that connect Gmail, Slack, and Jira — for example, routing a flagged email to a Slack project channel or creating a Jira ticket from an action-item email. Chapter 10's cross-application automation is grounded in Tracy's inbox-to-project-system workflow.

ch11-practical-agents.scenario_title: Weekly project status report from scattered sources
ch11-practical-agents.rationale: A multi-step status-report agent — plan reads Jira, then Confluence, then Slack, then drafts the report, then halts for Tracy's review — is the natural agent design exercise for Chapter 11. This is the autonomous version of the Chapter 6 skill, now with a planner, sub-tasks, and escalation logic.

ch12-orchestrations-scheduled-work.scenario_title: Weekly project status report from scattered sources
ch12-orchestrations-scheduled-work.rationale: Chapter 12's recurring automation is Tracy's weekly status report workflow scheduled for every Monday morning — automatically aggregating the past week's Jira, Confluence, Slack, and transcript data and halting for her review before distribution. This is the highest-value recurring automation in her portfolio.

ch13-ask-user-question.scenario_title: ServiceNow ticket integration into weekly reports
ch13-ask-user-question.rationale: ServiceNow ticket integration requires multiple clarification and approval checkpoints: which project's tickets to include, which priority threshold warrants escalation, and whether the merged report is ready to send. Chapter 13's AskUserQuestion patterns are taught through these decision points in the ticket workflow.

ch14-debugging-improving-automations.scenario_title: ServiceNow ticket integration into weekly reports
ch14-debugging-improving-automations.rationale: The ServiceNow integration is a realistic debugging candidate — query results may be incomplete, ticket status classifications may be wrong, or the merged report section may be malformed. Chapter 14's troubleshooting checklist is applied to diagnosing and fixing these automation failures.

ch15-security-safety-governance.scenario_title: ServiceNow ticket integration into weekly reports
ch15-security-safety-governance.rationale: ServiceNow contains sensitive client service request data and incident records. Chapter 15's data privacy, permissions, and governance principles are grounded in the risks of automating ServiceNow access — prompt injection from ticket content, unauthorized data exposure, and output review before distribution.

ch16-personal-automation-system.scenario_title: Weekly project status report from scattered sources
ch16-personal-automation-system.rationale: Chapter 16's synthesis exercise asks Tracy to design her personal automation portfolio. The status report workflow anchors the portfolio as the 'flagship' automation — illustrating how skills (ch06), agents (ch11), scheduling (ch12), and governance (ch15) combine into a sustainable operating model.

ch01-intro-to-cowork-automation.artifact: a partially completed weekly executive status report for one of her two programs, showing which sections she currently fills in manually from Jira, Confluence, Slack, and meeting transcripts

ch02-automation-mindset.artifact: a raw Google Meet transcript from her Monday stand-up, annotated with the manual steps she takes to extract decisions and action items

ch03-setting-up-claude.artifact: her Claude workspace folder structure — organized session outputs, saved prompts, and a reference document pointing to her Gmail inbox triage workflow

ch04-context-management.artifact: a context package for the Google Drive reorganization task — a reference document listing the current folder structure and target naming convention

ch05-claude-md.artifact: a CLAUDE.MD file encoding her meeting summary standards: action item format, decision attribution rules, and Advisor360° project naming conventions

ch06-creating-skills.artifact: the Status Report Aggregation skill — a reusable Claude skill that accepts Jira board data, Confluence page content, Slack channel summary, and two transcript excerpts, then produces an executive summary and detailed supporting notes

ch07-evaluating-skills.artifact: an evaluation rubric applied to the output of her transcript summarization skill — checking accuracy of action item attribution, completeness of decision capture, format compliance, and absence of hallucinated risks

ch08-connecting-tools.artifact: a business tool integration map showing Gmail, Google Drive, Jira, and Slack connected to Claude Cowork, with a safe import checklist applied to the Gmail plugin

ch09-playwright-browser-automation.artifact: a browser automation workflow that navigates Tracy's Google Drive, reads folder metadata, and proposes a rename and reorganization plan — with a human-review checkpoint before any file is moved

ch10-zapier-saas-automation.artifact: a Zapier workflow that triggers on a Gmail label applied by Claude's triage automation and creates a Jira ticket in the appropriate project board for emails classified as action-required

ch11-practical-agents.artifact: a weekly report agent design document — goal statement, sub-task list (read Jira, read Confluence, read Slack, draft report, halt for review), escalation rules, and scope boundaries

ch12-orchestrations-scheduled-work.artifact: a scheduled Monday-morning automation that triggers her weekly report agent at 7:00 AM, collects the previous week's data, and delivers a review-ready draft to her Gmail inbox by 8:00 AM

ch13-ask-user-question.artifact: a ServiceNow ticket integration workflow with three AskUserQuestion checkpoints: (1) confirm ticket query scope, (2) approve escalation classification, (3) approve merged report before send

ch14-debugging-improving-automations.artifact: a troubleshooting log for a broken ServiceNow ticket query — root cause analysis (overly broad ticket filter), fix applied (project-specific tag added), and before/after output comparison

ch15-security-safety-governance.artifact: Tracy's personal automation governance policy — defining which systems require human review before output distribution, which data fields must never be included in prompts, and who can approve new automation deployments on her team

ch16-personal-automation-system.artifact: Tracy's personal automation operating model — a portfolio plan listing 5 reusable skills, 2 recurring orchestrations, 1 browser automation, 1 SaaS automation, and 1 agent workflow, each mapped to a weekly time savings estimate

prior_knowledge_assumed: ServiceNow ITSM, portal release, workflow automation
Slack team communication and channel management
Project and program management (Agile, milestones, risk, stakeholder reporting)
Cross-functional stakeholder communication and executive reporting

prior_knowledge_scaffolding_rules.assumed_no_introduction: All items in prior_knowledge_assumed MUST be referenced as known. Never introduce or define Jira, Confluence, ServiceNow, Slack, or Google Workspace — Tracy uses these daily.
scaffold_required: Claude Cowork skills — what they are and how to create/test them
Agent workflows with human oversight checkpoints
Prompt injection and safe automation governance
prior_knowledge_scaffolding_rules.scaffold_pattern: Concrete example from Tracy's work → analogy to known PM practice → formal definition (Concrete-Pictorial-Abstract)

cultural_and_locale_rules.idioms: Standard American English business register. No British spellings. No informal slang.
cultural_and_locale_rules.units: Imperial where relevant (though unlikely in this course)
